title: A/B Testing Model Responses
concept: Human evaluators are shown two blinded model responses (A and B) for the same prompt, selecting which response is superior.

exercises.prompt: What does A/B testing of model responses involve?

options.text: Testing if the AI knows the letters A and B.
Letting two robots fight in a physical arena.

exercises.explain: A/B testing uses human feedback to rank and choose the best AI response from two options.

cards.title: The AI Showdown
cards.body: When we build AI models, how do we know which one is truly better at chatting with humans? We can't just ask the AI! Instead, we set up a head-to-head match and let real humans pick the winner.

cards.title: What is A/B Testing?
cards.body: A/B Testing for AI models is a method where human judges compare two different, unlabeled responses (Response A and Response B) to the exact same prompt. The judge decides which response is better based on accuracy, helpfulness, and style.

cards.title: The Taste Test
cards.body: Imagine you are a judge at a baking competition. You are handed two unlabeled chocolate cookies: Cookie A and Cookie B. You don't know who baked them, so you can't be biased. You simply taste both and decide which one tastes better. That's exactly how we test AI models using humans!

cards.title: Comparing the Poems
cards.body:
A user asks: "Write a short poem about rain." 
Model A writes: "Water drops from the sky, wetting the ground."
Model B writes: "Clouds whisper secrets in drops of cool rain, washing the window and clearing the pain."
A human evaluator reviews both without knowing which AI wrote which, and chooses Model B's response as the winner.

cards.title: Myth: Computers Are the Best Judges
cards.body: You might think we use complex math formulas to decide which chatbot is best. While math is useful, human preferences are too complex for formulas alone. Real human judges are the gold standard for deciding which AI sounds more natural and helpful.

cards.title: Side-by-Side Comparison
cards.body: Let's look at how the same prompt gets sent to two different models, creating two options for the judge.

right.text: Creates Response B (e.g., a friendly, short reply)

left.text: Creates Response A (e.g., a very formal, long reply)

exercises.prompt: What is presented to a human evaluator during an AI A/B test?

options.text: One prompt, and two different model responses to compare.
Two different prompts, and only one model response.
A math test for the human to solve.
A chat history of two humans talking to each other.

exercises.explain: In A/B testing, the human evaluator is given two different outputs (A and B) generated from the exact same input prompt to see which one is better.

exercises.prompt: True or False: During A/B testing, the human evaluator should always know the name of the company that built each model.
exercises.explain: The evaluator should not know which model produced which response to prevent bias.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe how we hide model identities in tests.
exercises.sentence: To avoid bias, A/B testing must be ___ so judges do not know which model made which response.
exercises.explain: In A/B testing, the responses must be blinded (unlabeled) so the human judge isn't influenced by knowing which AI created which answer.

exercises.prompt: A human evaluator is asked to compare two responses. Which of the following parts of the test was written by a human BEFORE the AI generated any text?
exercises.explain: The prompt is the only human-written text here. Response A and Response B are generated by the two AI models being tested.

exercises.prompt: When doing an A/B test with human judges, why is it crucial to hide the names of the models (making it 'blinded')?

options.text: It helps the evaluator guess the name of the AI model.
It prevents bias, ensuring the judge rates the response purely on quality.
It makes the AI models run faster during the evaluation process.
It allows the AI models to see the human's rating in real time.

exercises.explain: A/B testing is double-blinded, meaning the human evaluator does not know which model generated which response. This prevents any bias towards a specific model and ensures the evaluation is based purely on the quality of the output.
